name: MiniMax M3
license: MiniMax Model Community License (non-commercial restrictions apply; HF license field shows 'minimax-community') — verify exact license text at https://huggingface.co/MiniMaxAI/MiniMax-M3/blob/main/LICENSE
archDisplay: Sparse Mixture-of-Experts with MiniMax Sparse Attention (MSA) for 1M-token context, native multimodal (mixed-modality pretraining from step one); 428B total / 23B active params
note: Successor to M2 introducing MiniMax Sparse Attention (MSA), giving a native 1M-token multimodal (text/image/video input) context window with reported 9x faster prefill / 15x faster decode vs M2 at 1M context. Open-weight release on Hugging Face alongside hosted API access. Official pricing page lists a base rate of $0.60/$2.40 per million input/output tokens with a standing 50% promotional discount to the effective $0.30/$1.20 used here for inputs ≤512K tokens; price doubles beyond 512K tokens and a 1.5x 'priority' service tier also exists (not modeled as a separate entry).

results.notes: Reported on the official model card as 'SWE-bench Verified'; mapped to the base swe-bench slug since no separate 'verified' slug exists.
Also corroborated by independent press coverage citing the same 59.0% SWE-bench Pro figure vs GPT-5.5/Gemini 3.1 Pro.
Reported on the official model card as 'MMMU Pro'.

Reported on the official model card as 'Video-MME v2'; mapped to the base videomme slug — flagging that this may be a newer/harder variant of the benchmark rather than the original Video-MME, so treat as approximate.
Stated directly on MiniMax's official M3 product page as surpassing Claude Opus 4.7 (79.3); also relayed by independent press (VentureBeat and others).

Terminal-Bench 2.1, read directly from the benchmark table embedded as figures/benchmark.jpeg on the official HF model card (decoded the image directly since the table isn't in the markdown text). Per the card's evaluation-methodology footnotes: tested on MiniMax's internal infra with an 8C16G sandbox, 2-hour timeout, 128K max output tokens, using Terminus 2 as scaffolding. Exact figure (66.0%) independently corroborated by MarkTechPost's June 2026 launch coverage.

OSWorld-Verified, from the same official model-card benchmark image. Per the card's methodology footnote: tested on 361 (of ~369) samples from a 'nogdrive' collection -- i.e. excluding Google-Drive-dependent tasks -- using the official OSWorld-Verified codebase, relative coordinates 0-1000, 1920x1080 resolution; flagged here as a near-complete-but-not-full-suite subset rather than the entire official benchmark. Note: independent press (MarkTechPost) cites a different figure, 70.06%, for what it labels the same benchmark; 75.2 is taken from MiniMax's own primary model card as the vendor-authoritative number and the discrepancy is flagged for the record rather than silently resolved.

USAMO 2026, reported as 36/42 (=85.7%) on the official model-card benchmark image. Per the card's methodology footnote, graded per MathArena's official evaluation approach: model proof output normalized, then graded by two independent judge models (GPT-5.4 and Gemini 3.1 Pro, both at high reasoning effort) using human-expert rubrics, with the minimum of the two judges' scores taken as final.

GPQA Diamond, independently run by Artificial Analysis as part of their Intelligence Index v4.1 suite (not MiniMax self-reported). ~93% per AA's article; corroborated at 92.9% by benchlm.ai's table explicitly citing Artificial Analysis as the source. Not previously tracked for M3 -- the self-reported HF-card benchmark image does not include GPQA. [score corrected during round-6 completeness verify: proposed 93 -> 92.9]
Humanity's Last Exam, independently run by Artificial Analysis. ~37% per AA's article; corroborated at 37.1% by benchlm.ai citing the same AA source. Not previously tracked for M3.
IFBench, independently run by Artificial Analysis. ~83% per AA's article; corroborated at 82.9% by benchlm.ai citing the same AA source. Not previously tracked for M3.

Macro success — Wilson 95% CI [18, 35] statistically matches OpenAI's flagship GPT-5.6 Sol (18%, CI [12, 27]) at a fraction of the price: the whole run cost $40, the cheapest of the field. Mean shaped reward 0.713. Per-slice exact successes /20: steward_7d 0, rulebook_7d 1, dedup 13, orienteering 7, gauntlet_7g 5. 26% budget-exhaustion rate, disclosed under the benchmark's >15% prominent-disclosure rule.
Last on the NPP anytime ladder. Exact success 0/20, as preregistered for every model.

verificationNotes:
Confirmed released via official Hugging Face model repo (MiniMaxAI/MiniMax-M3, actively downloaded), MiniMax's own API docs, Artificial Analysis, and independent press (MarkTechPost, VentureBeat, TechTimes) covering the June 1, 2026 launch and the accompanying MSA architecture arXiv paper (2606.13392, June 11, 2026). Param counts (428B/23B) independently corroborated by Artificial Analysis and multiple press writeups; one press summary confused this with an unrelated 109B experimental MSA-paper model — the 428B/23B figures are the ones consistently tied to the actual MiniMax-M3 checkpoint.

2026-07-16: Checked all 6 flagged-missing core benchmarks (mmlu, math, aime, humaneval, livecodebench, mmmu) for MiniMax M3 across official materials (HF model card including the decoded figures/benchmark.jpeg table, GitHub README, MiniMax's official m3 blog post and product page), independent leaderboards (Artificial Analysis's MiniMax-M3 article/Intelligence Index suite; benchlm.ai's dedicated MiniMax-M3 page, which lists ~30 benchmarks M3 has published scores for, plus benchlm's separate mmluPro/aime2025/liveCodeBench/mmmu leaderboard tables; vals.ai has no MiniMax-M3 page), and sibling models MiniMax M2 / M2.7 already in this corpus for cross-reference -- none report a standalone MMLU, MATH/MATH-500, AIME, HumanEval, LiveCodeBench, or MMMU (non-Pro) score for M3. MiniMax's own 'PostTrainBench' composite (37.1%, per minimax.io/models/text/m3) aggregates sub-tasks including AIME2025, GPQA Main, GSM8K and HumanEval but publishes only the blended score, not per-task breakouts, so it cannot back out an individual AIME or HumanEval figure. This tracks with MiniMax's shift for M3 toward agentic/coding-suite benchmarks (SWE-bench, Terminal-Bench, MCP Atlas, OSWorld, USAMO) and Artificial Analysis's modern Intelligence Index suite (GPQA, HLE, IFBench), superseding the legacy MMLU/MATH/AIME/HumanEval/LiveCodeBench/MMMU battery that predecessors M2/M2.7 still partly reported (M2: AIME25 78, MMLU-Pro 82, LiveCodeBench 83; M2.7: AIME 94.2, MMLU-Pro 81.8) -- treated as a genuine, deliberate non-publication for M3 rather than a research gap; all six left unadded pending a future official disclosure.

2026-07-17 (round-22 deep second-pass, new source types only): Re-chased the mmlu/math/aime/humaneval gaps using six source types the prior round did NOT check (official materials/Epoch AI/Vals.ai/LMArena/BenchLM were already exhausted). Results below.

1) Stanford HELM (crfm.stanford.edu/helm — Classic, Lite, Capabilities). The leaderboard tables are client-side/JS-rendered so direct fetch only returned page chrome; repeated targeted web search for any MiniMax model on HELM returned nothing. No evidence MiniMax models are currently part of HELM's evaluated roster at all. Ruled out for lack of any hit (not a positive confirmation of absence, but no path found to a MiniMax-M3 HELM result).

2) arXiv / Semantic Scholar. Found arXiv:2606.13392 ("MiniMax Sparse Attention" — the same paper already cited in this entry's specSources for archClass/archDisplay) contains Table 2 with MMLU 67.0/67.2/66.8, MMLU-Pro 38.5/38.8/39.1, GSM8K 76.2/77.7/73.7, and HumanEval 61.0/64.0/57.9 across "Full/MSA-PT/MSA-CPT" variants. Fetched the full HTML (arxiv.org/html/2606.13392v1) and confirmed these numbers belong to a 109B-total/6B-active-parameter research ablation model trained under a 3T-token budget to isolate the MSA mechanism — explicitly NOT the shipped 428B/23B MiniMax-M3 checkpoint, which the paper only links to (HF repo) without repeating benchmark numbers. This is precisely the "109B experimental MSA-paper model" mix-up this corpus's own verificationNotes already warns that some press outlets fell into — reconfirmed here and deliberately not used. Semantic Scholar's API returned 429 (rate-limited) on two attempts; broader arXiv-scoped web search surfaced no other paper citing "MiniMax-M3" as a baseline with any of the four target scores.

3) GitHub eval-harness run logs. No GitHub repo with a MiniMax-M3 lm-evaluation-harness results.json was found. github.com/vllm-project/vllm issue #45562 (MiniMax-M3-MXFP8 ROCm KV-cache bug) contains only a GSM8K regression check (0.0099 broken / 0.9575 patched / 0.9666 BF16 baseline) — no MMLU/MATH/AIME/HumanEval. github.com/LiveBench/LiveBench's README has no MiniMax reference; github.com/xigh/open-weight-models (a curated open-weight benchmark list) has an M2.7 entry but no M3 entry at all. Closest hit, on Hugging Face rather than GitHub proper: the community quantization card Mapika/MiniMax-M3-NVFP4 states results were "measured via lm-evaluation-harness against the official MiniMax-M3-MXFP8 endpoint as baseline, same engine (SGLang)" and reports MMLU (flan CoT few-shot, 25% sample) = 77.36 for the MXFP8 baseline (74.16 for its own NVFP4 quant), plus GSM8K 93.93/92.57 and GPQA-diamond (CoT zero-shot) 76.26/69.70. This card's GPQA-diamond baseline of 76.26 diverges by ~16-17 points from two independently-corroborating credible sources: Artificial Analysis's ~92.9-93% GPQA already in this entry's own results array, and NVIDIA's own nvidia/MiniMax-M3-NVFP4 card, which separately reports FP8-baseline GPQA Diamond = 92.53. Such a large internal inconsistency on the same benchmark/same nominal baseline model strongly suggests a methodology defect in Mapika's harness run (most plausibly reasoning/thinking-token truncation or a chat-template mismatch feeding a generic CoT-zero-shot prompt to a native "thinking" reasoning model) — which discredits every number from that run, including its MMLU=77.36. Deliberately declined to add this MMLU figure to the corpus as non-credible rather than propose a number with a known, unresolved internal contradiction.

4) Other leaderboards/aggregators. OpenRouter's benchmarks page (openrouter.ai/minimax/minimax-m3/benchmarks) shows no MMLU/MATH/AIME/HumanEval. LiveBench.ai: MiniMax M3 IS listed (per llm-stats.com's LiveBench mirror: rank #31, global score 0.700; per a separate aggregator citation: 69.75/100, rank #15 of 200, weakest category Agentic #93) but LiveBench's own task taxonomy (Math/Coding/Reasoning/Data-Analysis/Language/IF/Agentic-Coding) is a distinct, non-equivalent benchmark suite from the legacy Hendrycks-MATH/AIME/HumanEval/MMLU slugs tracked in this corpus, so even a per-category breakdown would not be a valid substitute score for these specific slugs (and the per-category breakdown wasn't even retrievable from the fetched view). Scale AI SEAL/Scale Labs leaderboard: found no MiniMax-M3-specific SEAL page; search only re-surfaced already-known SWE-bench Pro/Verified figures. llm-stats.com's own MiniMax-M3 page has MMLU/MATH/AIME/HumanEval-labeled sections in its site navigation but no scores are actually populated for M3 there. PapersWithCode: no MiniMax-M3 entries found on any MMLU/MATH/HumanEval leaderboard via search.

5) Non-English-language sources. Chinese coverage checked: SegmentFault's "非线智能测评" review, 302.AI's own "基准实验室" review (302.ai runs its own proprietary tests), 智东西, and MiniMax's own Chinese-language blog (minimaxi.com) — all discuss PostTrainBench (0.37 composite), BrowseComp (83.5), SWE-bench Pro, Claw-Eval, and OmniDocBench qualitatively or via already-known composite figures; none report standalone MMLU/MATH/AIME/HumanEval numbers. Korean coverage checked (myclaw.ai/ko/blog/minimax-m3): general release coverage, explicitly notes agentic evaluation requires going "beyond standard metrics" — no numbers given. Japanese-language search returned no Japanese-language sources at all (only English/Chinese aggregator pages), so no genuine Japanese blog/forum test was found to check.

6) Sibling models. Re-checked MiniMax-M1 (arXiv 2506.13585 — AIME2024 86.0% for M1-80k, a different/older sibling, not M2/M3), "The MiniMax-M2 Series" paper (arXiv 2605.26494), and multiple M2.5/M2.7-vs-M3 comparison pages (llmreference.com's M2.7-vs-M3 compare page, mindstudio.ai, iternal.ai's 2026 LLM selection guide, xigh/open-weight-models). None disclose M3's MMLU/MATH/AIME/HumanEval via comparison to a sibling — the llmreference.com M2.7-vs-M3 page explicitly states M3's release data doesn't publish MMLU/GPQA/HumanEval/LiveCodeBench; iternal.ai's guide gives M2.5 MMLU=80.2% and M2.7 SWE-bench/GDPval-AA figures but nothing for M3.

Conclusion: all 6 assigned new source types were genuinely checked. No credible standalone MMLU, MATH, AIME, or HumanEval score for the production 428B/23B MiniMax-M3 checkpoint was found anywhere. One near-miss candidate (Mapika/MiniMax-M3-NVFP4's lm-eval-harness MMLU=77.36) was found and deliberately rejected as non-credible due to a ~17-point internally-inconsistent GPQA discrepancy against two corroborating sources. All four gaps remain omitted_no_evidence -- this is now the second consecutive research round reaching this conclusion via non-overlapping source sets, reinforcing that MiniMax deliberately did not publish/run these legacy benchmarks for M3, and no independent party has filled the gap with a trustworthy number as of 2026-07-17.

2026-07-18 (round-23, third and final pass, new source types only): Re-chased mmlu/math/aime/humaneval using the 5 mandated new source types not exhausted by rounds 1-2 (official materials/Epoch AI/Vals.ai/LMArena/BenchLM/llm-stats.com in round 1; Stanford HELM/arXiv-SemanticScholar/GitHub eval-harness logs/MathArena/LiveBench/OpenRouter/Scale SEAL/regional-language/sibling-models in round 2). All 5 genuinely checked; findings below.

1) WAYBACK MACHINE. Blocked at the infrastructure level for the entire session: web.archive.org returned HTTP 429 ("Too Many Requests") on every attempt -- 2 via the WebFetch tool (which refused outright with "unable to fetch from web.archive.org") and 6 further direct curl attempts spaced over ~10 minutes (including a dedicated background Monitor poll loop). Confirmed this was specific to web.archive.org's playback service, not a general network fault: archive.org's root domain (a different service on the same top-level org) returned 200, as did google.com, in the same window. Could not retrieve either the minimax.io/models/text/m3 launch-day snapshot (confirmed to exist via archive.org's own availability API: snapshot timestamp 20260601131719) or any llm-stats.com/artificialanalysis.ai archived snapshots. Genuinely attempted per the task's instructions but yielded no content -- neither confirming nor ruling out a since-edited number.

2) CONFERENCE TALKS / KEYNOTES. Searched for an M3 launch keynote, dev-conference talk, or podcast. No dedicated MiniMax keynote video found -- the one prominent YouTube hit ("I Tested NEW Minimax M3 on Four Projects") is a third-party reviewer's own hands-on test, not an official presentation, and doesn't cite MMLU/MATH/AIME/HumanEval. Confirmed MiniMax exhibited M3 as a product showcase at WAIC 2026 (Shanghai, July 17-20) rather than delivering a dedicated technical keynote with new benchmark disclosures (WAIC's headline keynotes were Xi Jinping and Richard Sutton). No podcast appearance by MiniMax researchers discussing M3 benchmarks was found.

3) INVESTOR / PRESS MATERIALS. MiniMax Group (HKEX: 0100) IPO'd Jan 9, 2026; checked its investor-relations hub (ir.minimax.io), annual report/earnings coverage (BigGo Finance, S&P Global, kr-asia), and analyst notes (Quartr, AlphaSpread). These discuss ARR/revenue trajectory ($150M+ ARR, $219M 2026 revenue forecast per S&P Global) and only qualitatively reference "company-reported SWE-Bench Pro scores that edge out GPT-5.5" (techtimes.com) as evidence of M3's capability -- no MMLU/MATH/AIME/HumanEval figures appear in any investor-facing document found.

4) GITHUB ISSUES / SDK REPO DISCUSSIONS. Checked MiniMax-AI/MiniMax-M3's own issues tab (all 13 open issues are bug reports/feature requests -- API streaming bugs, quota accounting, a 128GB-workstation-sweet-spot request -- none discuss benchmark scores; the repo's Discussions tab is disabled entirely, HTTP 410). Cross-repo GitHub code+issue search surfaced a genuinely new, previously-unchecked SDK repo: NVIDIA/TensorRT-LLM, which maintains its own CI accuracy-regression test suite for MiniMax-M3 (`tests/integration/defs/accuracy/references/{mmlu,gsm8k}.yaml`, `TestMiniMaxM3` in test_llm_api_pytorch.py, added in PR #15292, extended in PR #15687 MXFP8 and PR #15857 NVFP4). This IS a real, independently-run MMLU + GSM8K evaluation against the actual production BF16/MXFP8/NVFP4 MiniMax-M3 checkpoints on Blackwell hardware -- but the yaml values are explicitly CI pass/fail *thresholds*, not measured scores: PR #15292's original GSM8K entry carried an explicit code comment stating "Threshold is set ~1pp below the measured TRT-LLM hardpath score (~88)" (threshold 87), and the MMLU threshold followed the same undocumented convention (originally 80, raised to 85 in PR #15687 for BF16/MXFP8, with a separate 83 threshold for the NVFP4 checkpoint in PR #15857/#16021). No PR description, code comment, or human review comment anywhere in this chain states the actual measured MMLU number -- only the deliberately-conservative floor. Per this corpus's own precedent (round 2's rejection of the Mapika NVFP4 card's lm-eval MMLU=77.36), a threshold is not a citable score, so this was NOT added despite being a highly credible, genuinely new source -- documented here as a strong near-miss rather than a usable number. Confirmed via the same repo that TensorRT-LLM's `humaneval.yaml` reference file exists but has no MiniMax entry, and that no `math.yaml` / `aime.yaml` file exists in the repo at all (404) -- i.e. TensorRT-LLM only ever evaluates MMLU+GSM8K for M3, never MATH/AIME/HumanEval, closing off this source for those three benchmarks definitively. Also checked vllm-project/vllm's MiniMax-M3 roadmap issue #45668 (kernel/feature checklist, no accuracy numbers), phaseoteam/Phaseo PR #605 (the "AI-Stats" community benchmark catalog, which added only swe-bench-pro/terminal-bench-2.1/browsecomp/scale-mcp-atlas/os-world for M3 -- same known set, corroborating the absence rather than adding to it), and amd/Quark + NVIDIA/Model-Optimizer changelogs (only quantization bug-fix notes referencing MiniMax-M3/M2, no benchmark figures).

5) THIRD-PARTY REVIEW/ANALYSIS SITES not yet checked. Simon Willison: no M3-specific post found (only general "ai-in-china" tag coverage of M2 and a comparison mentioning Opus 4.8/SWE-Bench Pro pricing, no MMLU/MATH/AIME/HumanEval). interconnects.ai (Nathan Lambert): no dedicated M3 post found in the archive search -- only M2/M2.5 coverage. ChinaTalk: only an M3 launch mention embedded in a broader Zhipu/MiniMax IPO article, no benchmark specifics. SemiAnalysis's InferenceX (inferencex.semianalysis.com, github.com/SemiAnalysisAI/InferenceX): a real, actively-maintained independent GPU-inference benchmark for M3, but it exclusively measures throughput/latency/cost-per-token across GPU vendors (B200 vs H100, B300 vs MI355X, EAGLE speculative-decoding speedup) -- zero accuracy/quality benchmarks, so out of scope for MMLU/MATH/AIME/HumanEval by design. benchable.ai's MiniMax-M3 page shows unsourced aggregate percentile categories ("Coding 95%, Mathematics 95%, Reasoning 96%") with no stated methodology or original-benchmark mapping -- explicitly declined to use these as MMLU/MATH proxies since neither the page nor any secondary source explains what these percentages actually measure. Also found (via GitHub code search, not deliberately targeted) an independent community MMLU-**Pro** run for "MiniMax-M3" in likefallwind/edubenchmark (12,032-item full set, 85.56% overall accuracy, math-subcategory 94.6%) -- this is MMLU-Pro, a distinct slug already tracked separately elsewhere in this corpus for sibling models, not the base MMLU this round is chasing, so left out of scope and not added.

Conclusion: all 5 mandated new source types were genuinely exercised (Wayback attempted but blocked by persistent 429s; the other 4 fully executed). The one substantive new finding -- NVIDIA TensorRT-LLM's own CI accuracy thresholds for MMLU/GSM8K against the real MiniMax-M3 checkpoint -- is credible as a signal that MiniMax-M3 scores somewhere in the mid-80s% region on standard MMLU, but is architecturally a regression floor rather than a reported measurement, so per the "no estimated numbers" rule it was documented, not added. No credible, precise, citable MMLU, MATH, AIME, or HumanEval score for the production 428B/23B MiniMax-M3 checkpoint was found. This is now the third consecutive independent research round (three non-overlapping source-type sets) reaching the same conclusion, which is about as strong as evidence of genuine, durable non-publication gets for an open corpus -- all four gaps remain omitted_no_evidence.

2026-07-18 (round-24, current-gen gap chase: livecodebench + mmmu): Modality check first -- M3's corpus record lists modalities [text, vision, video] and capabilities.vision=true, so MMMU is architecturally applicable (this is NOT a text-only model); the gap had to be genuinely researched, not dismissed. Checked 5 of the 6 mandated method categories (official launch materials; independent trackers; academic/arXiv; GitHub eval-harness logs; sibling-model comparison tables), all pointing to the same conclusion for both benchmarks.

1) Official launch materials. Re-fetched the raw HF README (huggingface.co/MiniMaxAI/MiniMax-M3/raw/main/README.md), the rendered HF model card, MiniMax's own product page (minimax.io/models/text/m3) and blog post (minimax.io/blog/minimax-m3), and a community-submitted HF discussion (#17, "Add evaluation results from model card benchmark graph") that specifically transcribed the figures/benchmark.jpeg image into structured data. None contain a LiveCodeBench row. None contain a base MMMU row -- only "MMMU Pro (Standard 10 Options)" = 78.1 (already tracked in this corpus as the separate mmmu-pro slug) plus Video-MME/Video-MMMU/OmniDocBench.

2) Independent trackers. benchlm.ai/models/minimax-m3 (which explicitly lists ~30 benchmark rows across Agentic/Coding/Multimodal categories) has no LiveCodeBench and no base-MMMU row -- multimodal section only has MMMU-Pro, OfficeQA Pro, OmniDocBench 1.5, VideoMMU, Video-MME. llm-stats.com/models/minimax-m3's benchmark section is unpopulated placeholder content as of the fetch (2026-07-18) with a "Scores sourced from..." disclaimer but zero rows rendered. artificialanalysis.ai/models/minimax-m3 and artificialanalysis.ai/articles/minimax-m3 (AA's Intelligence Index v4.1 suite -- GDPval-AA, Terminal-Bench, SciCode, HLE, GPQA Diamond, CritPt, AA-Omniscience, AA-LCR, IFBench, APEX-Agents, MMMU-Pro) do not include LiveCodeBench or base MMMU as suite components at all for this model generation -- AA's own composite has evidently dropped LiveCodeBench from its v4.1 methodology. A search of livecodebench.github.io/leaderboard.html and its HF Space mirror (huggingface.co/spaces/livecodebench/leaderboard) surfaced no MiniMax-M3 entry (comparison hits were for GLM-4.7/GLM-5.2/DeepSeek-V4 instead).

3) Academic/arXiv. arXiv:2606.13392 (MiniMax Sparse Attention paper, already cited in this entry's specSources) abstract/metadata makes no mention of LiveCodeBench or MMMU; per this corpus's round-22/23 findings, the paper's Table 2 numbers belong to a separate 109B/6B-active ablation model, not the shipped 428B/23B M3 checkpoint, and don't include either benchmark regardless.

4) GitHub eval-harness logs. No MiniMax-M3-specific lm-evaluation-harness run (official or community) surfaced with LiveCodeBench or MMMU results via targeted GitHub code/issue search; EleutherAI/lm-evaluation-harness's mmmu task exists only as a generic prototype harness feature, not a MiniMax-M3-specific run log.

5) Sibling-model comparison tables. This is the most decisive check: MiniMax M2 (sibling, already in this corpus) DOES report livecodebench=83 self-reported, and MiniMax-M1 reports livecodebench=65 -- so the corpus family clearly used to publish this benchmark. But every dedicated M2-vs-M3 / M2.7-vs-M3 comparison article checked (llmreference.com/compare/minimax-m2.7/minimax-m3, benchlm.ai/compare/minimax-m2-7-vs-minimax-m3, lushbinary.com's "M3 vs M2.7: What Changed" upgrade guide, myclaw.ai's "M2 vs M3" blog) either omits LiveCodeBench entirely from the comparison or states outright that M3 dropped it -- one fetched summary explicitly states: "The current M3 release material does not publish LiveCodeBench scores, so a direct LiveCodeBench comparison table between M2.7 and M3 is not available from official sources." For MMMU, sibling MiniMax-VL-01 (an older, smaller dedicated vision model already in this corpus) reports mmmu=68.5 and mmmu-pro=52.7, confirming the base-mmmu slug is a real, normally-populated field for this family's vision-capable models -- M3 is the outlier that only ever reports the harder Pro variant.

Conclusion: for both livecodebench and mmmu (base), the absence is corroborated across 5 independent method categories, including the strongest possible signal -- the model's own product family (M1, M2) and a same-family vision model (VL-01) publishing exactly these two scores while M3 conspicuously does not, and third-party comparison pages explicitly noting the drop. This is consistent with, and extends, this corpus's already-documented finding (rounds 21-23) that M3 deliberately shifted away from the legacy MMLU/MATH/AIME/HumanEval/LiveCodeBench/MMMU battery toward agentic/coding-suite and AA Intelligence-Index-style benchmarks. Both gaps left as omitted_no_evidence -- no credible number found, and no estimate proposed.
